title: Let the units audit the calculation

objectives.statement: Distinguish a physical quantity from its numerical value and unit, and interpret compound units as algebraic products or quotients.
Construct unit-factor chains from stated equalities so unwanted units cancel and the target unit remains.
Convert areas, volumes, and compound rates by applying conversion factors to every occurrence and power of a unit.
Use dimensions and magnitude checks to reject invalid operations, equations, or reported results.

scenes.title: A tidy number with the wrong unit
scenes.contentHtml:
A tidy number with the wrong unit

A fictional pump moves liquid at 18 L/min for 40 s. A dashboard multiplies 18 by 40 and reports 720 L.

The arithmetic is tidy, but the unit audit fails. Multiplying the written quantities gives

18Lmin×40 s=720L⋅smin,18\frac{\text{L}}{\text{min}}\times40\text{ s}=720\frac{\text{L}\cdot\text{s}}{\text{min}},

not litres. Seconds and minutes have not cancelled. Converting 40 s40\text{ s} to 2/3 min2/3\text{ min} gives 12 L12\text{ L}.

This lesson makes units an active error detector. For each calculation you will:

  1. name the quantity sought;
  2. multiply by conversion factors equal to one;
  3. cancel units before trusting the number;
  4. challenge the final dimension and magnitude.

The settings and values are fictional. The lesson develops mathematical unit reasoning, not dosing, clinical decision-making, measurement uncertainty, or laboratory procedure.

Starting check

Without calculating, decide whether each target is possible.

  • Multiplying m/s by s to obtain m.
  • Adding 4 kg to 3 s.
  • Converting 2 m² to square centimetres with only one factor of 100 cm/m.

If cancellation is unfamiliar, write every compound unit as a fraction and cross out only identical factors that occur once above and once below the fraction bar.

Source Note
claims claim-quantity-value-unit, claim-quantity-equations-independentsources source-nist-si-chapter7

NIST distinguishes a quantity from its numerical value and unit and explains that quantity equations are independent of the units chosen. The pump story, numbers, error, and teaching loop are original.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every cancellation is stated in words as well as symbols. No task depends on color, dragging, timing, or a visual crossing-out gesture. A screen-reader-friendly alternative is to say: “the second unit in the numerator cancels the second unit in the denominator.”

scenes.title: Treat units as part of the algebra
scenes.contentHtml:
Treat units as part of the algebra

A measured or specified quantity can be written conceptually as

quantity=numerical value×unit.\text{quantity}=\text{numerical value}\times\text{unit}.

The same quantity can have different numerical values in different units: 1 m=100 cm1\text{ m}=100\text{ cm}. The number changes; the length does not.

Definition
id definition-conversion-factor

A conversion factor is a ratio of equal quantities and therefore has value one, such as 100 cm/1 m100\text{ cm}/1\text{ m}. Multiplying by it changes the unit representation without changing the underlying quantity.

Orientation matters. To convert 2.5 m2.5\text{ m} to centimetres, choose the form that cancels metres:

2.5 m×100 cm1 m=250 cm.2.5\text{ m}\times\frac{100\text{ cm}}{1\text{ m}}=250\text{ cm}.

The reciprocal is also one, but it points toward metres rather than away from them.

Compound units

Read km/h as kilometres divided by hours. A chain can contain several factors:

72kmh×1000 m1 km×1 h3600 s=20ms.72\frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} \times\frac{1000\text{ m}}{1\text{ km}} \times\frac{1\text{ h}}{3600\text{ s}} =20\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}.

Kilometres cancel; hours cancel; metres per second remain. The surviving unit answers “what kind of quantity did this chain produce?”

Dimensions as a structural test

Units are conventional labels; dimensions describe broad quantity types such as length [L][L], time [T][T], and mass [M][M]. Speed has dimension [L][T]−1[L][T]^{-1} and area has [L]2[L]^2.

An equation can be numerically evaluated yet still be structurally impossible. You may add 3 m+2 m3\text{ m}+2\text{ m}, but 3 m+2 s3\text{ m}+2\text{ s} has no single resulting dimension. Likewise, distance d=vtd=vt passes because ([L][T]−1)([T])=[L]([L][T]^{-1})([T])=[L].

Dimensional agreement is necessary, not sufficient: the wrong equation can still have the right dimensions. It is an audit, not proof.

Source Note
claims claim-quantity-value-unit, claim-conversion-preserves-quantity, claim-quantity-equations-independent, claim-unit-writingsources source-nist-si-chapter7

NIST documents number-times-unit quantity values, unit-independent quantity equations, and clear unit-symbol writing. The factor-chain derivations, dimension labels, and examples are original synthesis.

scenes.title: Build a chain that explains itself
scenes.contentHtml:
Build a chain that explains itself

A fictional cart travels at 54 km/h54\text{ km/h} for 25 s25\text{ s}. Find the distance in metres.

Worked Example
id worked-example-cart

1. Name the target. Distance in metres.

2. Write the quantity relation. d=vtd=vt. Its dimensions pass: ([L]/[T])([T])=[L]([L]/[T])([T])=[L].

3. Build the chain. Convert the speed while retaining its meaning:

54kmh×1000 m1 km×1 h3600 s=15ms.54\frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} \times\frac{1000\text{ m}}{1\text{ km}} \times\frac{1\text{ h}}{3600\text{ s}} =15\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}.

4. Combine with time.

15ms×25 s=375 m.15\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\times25\text{ s}=375\text{ m}.

5. Challenge it. At roughly 15 m/s15\text{ m/s} for roughly 25 s25\text{ s}, several hundred metres is plausible. Dividing 375 m375\text{ m} by 25 s25\text{ s} recovers 15 m/s15\text{ m/s}.

6. Interpret cautiously. The computed fictional distance is 375 m375\text{ m} under the stated constant-speed model. Unit consistency does not establish that the speed was actually constant or accurately measured.

Check
id check-chain-direction

Why must hours appear in the numerator of the second time factor, 1 h/3600 s1\text{ h}/3600\text{ s}?

Because the original hour is in the denominator of km/h. One hour above and one hour below cancel, leaving seconds below.

Source Note
claims claim-conversion-preserves-quantity, claim-quantity-equations-independentsources source-nist-si-chapter7

The example applies NIST's distinction between quantities and their unit-dependent numerical values. Its model, values, scaffold, and checks are original.

scenes.title: A squared unit needs a squared factor
scenes.contentHtml:
A squared unit needs a squared factor

Suppose a fictional sensor pad has area 0.32 m20.32\text{ m}^2. A tempting conversion is

0.32 m2×100 cm1 m=32 m⋅cm.0.32\text{ m}^2\times\frac{100\text{ cm}}{1\text{ m}}=32\text{ m}\cdot\text{cm}.

The number 32 may look familiar, but the unit audit exposes the problem: only one of the two metre factors cancelled.

Misconception
id misconception-powered-unit

The relation 1 m=100 cm1\text{ m}=100\text{ cm} applies to every length factor. Because m2=m×m\text{m}^2=\text{m}\times\text{m}, square the entire conversion factor:

0.32 m2×(100 cm1 m)2=0.32×10,000 cm2=3200 cm2.0.32\text{ m}^2 \times\left(\frac{100\text{ cm}}{1\text{ m}}\right)^2 =0.32\times10{,}000\text{ cm}^2 =3200\text{ cm}^2.

For a cubed unit, cube the factor. For a rate, convert each numerator and denominator unit in the power where it occurs.

A quick magnitude check agrees: one square metre contains a 100100-by-100100 grid of square centimetres, so it contains 10,000 cm210{,}000\text{ cm}^2, not 100 cm2100\text{ cm}^2.

Source Note
claims claim-conversion-preserves-quantity, claim-powered-unitssources source-nist-si-chapter7

The number-times-unit relation supports applying a linear equality to each unit factor. The pad, grid explanation, and misconception repair are original mathematical synthesis.

scenes.title: Choose, orient, cancel, check
scenes.contentHtml:
Choose, orient, cancel, check

Attempt each item before reading its feedback. On paper or in plain text, show four lines: target, factors, surviving unit, magnitude check.

1. One factor

Convert 4.6 kg4.6\text{ kg} to grams using 1 kg=1000 g1\text{ kg}=1000\text{ g}.

Feedback: 4.6 kg(1000 g/1 kg)=4600 g4.6\text{ kg}(1000\text{ g}/1\text{ kg})=4600\text{ g}. If kilograms remain, reverse the factor.

2. A denominator conversion

Convert 3.0 L/min3.0\text{ L/min} to litres per second.

Feedback: 3.0 L/min(1 min/60 s)=0.050 L/s3.0\text{ L/min}(1\text{ min}/60\text{ s})=0.050\text{ L/s}. A per-second number should be smaller because each second is a smaller time interval than a minute.

3. Powered units

Convert 2.5 cm32.5\text{ cm}^3 to cubic millimetres using 1 cm=10 mm1\text{ cm}=10\text{ mm}.

Feedback: 2.5 cm3(10 mm/1 cm)3=2500 mm32.5\text{ cm}^3(10\text{ mm}/1\text{ cm})^3=2500\text{ mm}^3. Using only 10 leaves uncancelled square centimetres.

4. Dimensional audit

A proposed model is x=atx=at, where xx is distance, aa has unit m/s², and tt has unit s. Can it be the complete equation for distance?

Feedback: No. The right side has m/s, the dimension of speed. Dimensional inconsistency proves the proposed equation cannot be complete. Adding another factor of time would repair the dimension but would not by itself prove a particular physical model.

Recovery route

  • Target unit cancels: invert the relevant factor.
  • Mixed units remain: the chain is incomplete.
  • Correct unit but implausible scale: inspect prefixes and denominators.
  • Squared or cubed unit remains partly uncancelled: apply the factor to the full power.
  • Dimensions agree but context still seems wrong: remember that the audit is necessary, not sufficient.
Source Note
claims claim-conversion-preserves-quantity, claim-quantity-equations-independent, claim-powered-unitssources source-nist-si-chapter7

The practice uses the documented quantity/unit framework. All prompts, values, feedback, and recovery routes are original.

scenes.title: Audit an unfamiliar transport calculation
scenes.contentHtml:
Audit an unfamiliar transport calculation

A fictional storage system moves data at 1.8 GB/min1.8\text{ GB/min} for 2.5 h2.5\text{ h}. Its log claims that 270 GB270\text{ GB} moved. Treat the decimal relations 1 h=60 min1\text{ h}=60\text{ min} and 1 GB=1000 MB1\text{ GB}=1000\text{ MB} as definitions for this task.

Determine the transferred amount in megabytes and audit the log.

Build your response

  1. Name the target quantity and unit.
  2. Write one continuous factor chain.
  3. Show every cancellation in words or symbols.
  4. Compare your result with the log and perform a magnitude check.

Feedback after a complete attempt

1.8GBmin×2.5 h×60 min1 h×1000 MB1 GB=270,000 MB.1.8\frac{\text{GB}}{\text{min}} \times2.5\text{ h} \times\frac{60\text{ min}}{1\text{ h}} \times\frac{1000\text{ MB}}{1\text{ GB}} =270{,}000\text{ MB}.

Minutes cancel minutes, hours cancel hours, and gigabytes cancel gigabytes. The equivalent amount is 270 GB270\text{ GB}, so the fictional log is consistent with the stated rate and duration.

A rough check gives (2 GB/min)(150 min)≈300 GB(2\text{ GB/min})(150\text{ min})\approx300\text{ GB}, close to 270 GB. This validates arithmetic consistency, not the truth of the fictional measurements.

If you multiplied 1.81.8 by 2.52.5 and stopped, your result retained GB·h/min; the unit showed that a conversion was still missing.

Source Note
claims claim-conversion-preserves-quantity, claim-quantity-equations-independentsources source-nist-si-chapter7

The transfer problem is an original fictional application of unit-independent quantity relations and unit-dependent numerical values.

scenes.title: Make the units your error detector
scenes.contentHtml:
Make the units your error detector

Reconstruct the method without looking back.

  1. Target: name the quantity and final unit.
  2. Relate: write the quantity equation before inserting numbers.
  3. Factor: multiply by ratios of equal quantities, oriented so unwanted units cancel.
  4. Power: apply a conversion to every occurrence of a squared or cubed unit.
  5. Audit: require the target dimension, then check magnitude or reverse the calculation.

Retrieval

  • Why does multiplying by 100 cm/1 m100\text{ cm}/1\text{ m} not change a length?
  • Why is 3 m+2 s3\text{ m}+2\text{ s} invalid even though both numbers can be entered in a calculator?
  • Why does converting square metres require squaring the linear factor?
  • What can dimensional agreement establish, and what can it not establish?

Then complete assessment.json. Mastery requires correct numbers and visible unit reasoning. If factors are oriented incorrectly, return to “Treat units as part of the algebra.” If powers are missed, return to the pad example. If a correct number carries no unit trail, redo the transfer task with the four response lines.

No conversion table needs to be memorized here. In later science work, use authoritative definitions supplied by the problem or field, then let the cancellation expose whether you used them coherently.

Source Note
claims claim-quantity-value-unit, claim-conversion-preserves-quantity, claim-quantity-equations-independent, claim-powered-units, claim-unit-writingsources source-nist-si-chapter7

The synthesis reflects NIST's quantity/unit distinctions and clear value notation. The five-step method and recovery routing are original and do not claim measured learning effectiveness.

The recorded digest covers the discloseable material task instructions, not hidden system or provider instructions.

assessment.title: Units and dimensional analysis mastery gate

items.prompt: Which factor correctly begins converting 7.5 m to centimetres?

answer.summary: Use 100 cm / 1 m so metres cancel and centimetres remain.
answer.reasoning: The ratio equals one because 100 cm and 1 m are equal quantities. Its denominator cancels the original metre unit.
commonErrors: Choosing the reciprocal without checking the surviving unit
Treating the equality as an instruction to divide regardless of target

items.prompt: Convert 180 m/min to m/s. Enter the numerical value in m/s.

answer.reasoning: Multiply by 1 min/60 s. Minutes cancel and 180/60 = 3.
commonErrors: Multiplying by 60 because the denominator unit was not tracked

items.prompt: Convert 0.45 m² to cm². Show the powered conversion factor and cancellation.

answer.correct: 0.45 m² × (100 cm / 1 m)² = 4500 cm²; both metre factors cancel.
answer.reasoning: A square metre contains two length factors, so the linear conversion factor is squared: 0.45 × 100² = 4500.
acceptableResponses: 4500 cm² with an equivalent expanded two-factor chain
commonErrors: Leaving a mixed unit such as m·cm

items.prompt: A quantity E has dimension M·L²/T², m has dimension M, and v has dimension L/T. Which expression is dimensionally compatible with E?

answer.reasoning: Squaring v gives L²/T²; multiplying by m supplies M. Dimensional compatibility does not prove that E equals mv² in a particular model.
commonErrors: Selecting an expression based only on familiar symbols

items.prompt: Explain why a dimensionally consistent equation can still be wrong. Give one concrete mathematical example.

answer.correct: Dimensions test structural compatibility, not numerical constants, signs, assumptions, or the chosen model. For example, d = vt and d = 10vt both have dimension L, but the second is not generally the distance traveled at constant speed v for time t.
answer.summary: Dimensional consistency is necessary but not sufficient.
answer.reasoning: Many distinct expressions share the same dimension. The audit can rule out mismatches but cannot select a unique physical relation.
acceptableResponses: Any valid example of two dimensionally matched equations where at least one has an incorrect constant, sign, or model
commonErrors: Giving an example whose two sides have different dimensions

items.prompt: A fictional coating line covers 2.4 m²/min for 45 s. Determine the covered area in cm². Show the quantity relation, a continuous conversion chain, cancellations, and a magnitude check.

answer.correct: 2.4 m²/min × 45 s × (1 min/60 s) × (100 cm/1 m)² = 18,000 cm². Seconds, minutes, and both metre factors cancel. Since 45 s is 0.75 min, the area is 1.8 m², and 1.8 × 10,000 = 18,000 cm².
answer.reasoning: Rate times time gives area. The time chain converts seconds to minutes, and the squared length factor converts both dimensions of area.
acceptableResponses: 1.8 m² followed by a separately shown squared conversion to 18000 cm², with valid cancellations and check
commonErrors: Applying the centimetre factor only once
Giving a correct number without a unit trail or magnitude check

criteria.description: Selects the equality ratio that cancels the starting unit and leaves the target unit.
Uses the reciprocal time factor with correct cancellation.
Squares or repeats the full linear conversion factor.
Cancels both metre factors and obtains 4500 cm².
Combines the declared dimensions to identify mv² and preserves the audit's limits.
States that agreement is necessary but not sufficient.
Provides a dimensionally valid example that differs by a wrong constant, sign, or model.
Uses area equals area-rate times time.
Builds an oriented continuous chain with complete cancellation.
Uses 0.75 min or 1.8 m² as an independent magnitude check.

sources.title: NIST Guide to the SI, Chapter 7: Rules and Style Conventions for Expressing Values of Quantities
creators: National Institute of Standards and Technology
sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
sources.version: Web presentation modified 2025-08-18; based on NIST Special Publication 811
sources.locator: Sections 7.1, 7.2, and 7.11 on quantity values, unit symbols, quantity equations, and numerical-value equations
sources.license: NIST Technical Series/public information; U.S. government work under NIST's Copyrights & Disclaimers and technical-series licensing statements, subject to attribution and third-party exceptions

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: The lesson uses factual quantity-and-unit conventions and independently creates all explanations, examples, chains, prompts, and assessments. No NIST wording, table, image, or teaching sequence is reproduced or adapted.

agentAccess.notes: The NIST copyright/disclaimer, technical-series licensing, and robots routes were checked before substantive access. The public page required no login, paywall, access-control bypass, or bulk retrieval.

claims.statement: The value of a quantity is expressed as the product of a numerical value and a unit, so the same quantity can have different numerical values when expressed in different units.
claims.scope: Introductory treatment of scalar physical quantities and coherent unit representations.
claims.uncertainty: This distinction does not address measurement uncertainty or vector direction.

claims.statement: A ratio of equal quantity values can serve as a multiplicative conversion factor equal to one, changing the numerical value and unit representation without changing the quantity.
claims.scope: Exact unit equalities stated in the lesson's mathematical tasks.
claims.uncertainty: Empirical or rounded conversion factors may introduce uncertainty outside this lesson's exact definitions.

claims.statement: A quantity equation is independent of the units used to express its quantities, whereas an equation among numerical values depends on the chosen units.
claims.scope: Quantity equations and numerical-value equations in introductory quantitative science.
claims.uncertainty: Dimensional agreement does not establish that an equation is the correct model.

claims.statement: When a unit is raised to a power, a unit conversion must be applied to that full power so every occurrence of the original unit cancels.
claims.scope: Algebraic conversions of squared and cubed units using exact linear equalities.
claims.uncertainty: This does not address affine temperature-scale conversions, which cannot generally be handled as simple multiplicative factors.

claims.statement: Clear quantity notation associates numerical values unambiguously with their unit symbols and places a space between a numerical value and most unit symbols.
claims.scope: SI-style writing used in this lesson, with the documented exceptions for plane-angle symbols outside scope.
claims.uncertainty: Discipline-specific typography and non-SI style guides can impose additional conventions.

terms.definition: A measurable or specified property whose value can be expressed as a numerical value multiplied by a unit.
The number multiplying a unit when a quantity is expressed in that unit.
A ratio of equal quantities used as a multiplicative factor of one to change unit representation without changing the quantity.
A description of a quantity type in terms of base quantity types such as length, time, and mass, independent of a particular unit.
The requirement that quantities added or equated have compatible dimensions; satisfying it is necessary but does not prove a model is correct.
A unit formed by multiplying, dividing, or exponentiating other units, such as metres per second or square centimetres.

Original synthesis and source use

The unit-audit structure, pump opening, cart example, sensor-pad misconception, storage transfer, coating-line assessment, all numerical values, prompts, feedback, recovery routes, and assessment logic are original course material. Every applied setting is explicitly fictional.

The NIST Chapter 7 record is used only as factual authority for the distinction among quantity, numerical value, and unit; clear unit notation; and unit independence of quantity equations. No NIST wording, organization, examples, figures, tables, or other expression is copied or adapted.

Rights and access

Before substantive access on 2026-07-22, NIST's Copyrights & Disclaimers page, technical-series licensing statement, and public robots route were checked. No source-specific agent prohibition, login, paywall, or access-control barrier was found. No third-party asset, dataset, question bank, patient material, or media is included.

Boundaries

This lesson teaches mathematical unit reasoning. It does not provide clinical advice, dosing guidance, experimental procedure, measurement-validity judgments, or formal uncertainty analysis. This authoring run creates no review or adjudication artifact and makes no claim of approval, publication, merge readiness, learner effectiveness, university credit, or clinical competence.
